[Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), [Konin](/wiki/Konin "Konin"), [Suceava](/wiki/Suceava "Suceava"), [Nazareth Illit](/wiki/Nazareth_Illit "Nazareth Illit"), [Saskatoon](/wiki/Saskatoon "Saskatoon"), [Klagenfurt](/wiki/Klagenfurt "Klagenfurt")

* [Chernivtsi University](/wiki/Chernivtsi_University "Chernivtsi University") * [Art Museum](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Regional_Art_Museum "Chernivtsi Regional Art Museum") * German People's House * Hotel Pension City * Olha Kobylians'ka Street * Theatre Square | |

([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Чернівці, pronounced [[tʃern⁽ʲ⁾iu̯ˈts⁽ʲ⁾i]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Uk-%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96.ogg "File:Uk-Чернівці.ogg"); [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Cernăuți*, pronounced [[tʃernəˈutsʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ro-Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi.ogg "File:Ro-Cernăuți.ogg"); see also [other names](#Names_and_etymology)) is a city in southwestern [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") on the upper course of the [Prut](/wiki/Prut "Prut") River. Formerly the capital of the historic region of [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), which is now divided between [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and Ukraine, Chernivtsi serves as the administrative center for the [Chernivtsi urban hromada](/wiki/Chernivtsi_urban_hromada "Chernivtsi urban hromada"),[[5]](#cite_note-reform2020-5) the [Chernivtsi Raion](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Raion "Chernivtsi Raion"), and the [oblast itself](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast"). The Chernivtsi population is 264,298 (2022 estimate),[[6]](#cite_note-ua2022estimate-6) and the latest [census in 2001](/wiki/Ukrainian_Census_(2001) "Ukrainian Census (2001)") was 240,600.[[7]](#cite_note-Census-7)

For the Israeli writer, see [Yemima Avidar-Tchernovitz](/wiki/Yemima_Avidar-Tchernovitz "Yemima Avidar-Tchernovitz"). For the former administrative subdivision of Romania, see [Cernăuți County](/wiki/Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi_County "Cernăuți County"). For other uses, see [Chernivtsi (disambiguation)](/wiki/Chernivtsi_(disambiguation) "Chernivtsi (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Ukraine._Pokutia._Beuaplan_1648.jpg/250px-Ukraine._Pokutia._Beuaplan_1648.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ukraine._Pokutia._Beuaplan_1648.jpg)

"Czarnowce" on a 1639 [Beauplan](/wiki/Guillaume_Le_Vasseur_de_Beauplan "Guillaume Le Vasseur de Beauplan") map centered on [Pokuttia](/wiki/Pokuttia "Pokuttia"); placed in "Wallachia or Little Moldavia", bottom right

**Chernivtsi** ([Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Чернівці, pronounced [[tʃern⁽ʲ⁾iu̯ˈts⁽ʲ⁾i]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Ukrainian "Help:IPA/Ukrainian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Uk-%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96.ogg "File:Uk-Чернівці.ogg"); [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Cernăuți*, pronounced [[tʃernəˈutsʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ro-Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi.ogg "File:Ro-Cernăuți.ogg"); see also [other names](#Names_and_etymology)) is a city in southwestern [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") on the upper course of the [Prut](/wiki/Prut "Prut") River. Formerly the capital of the historic region of [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), which is now divided between [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and Ukraine, Chernivtsi serves as the administrative center for the [Chernivtsi urban hromada](/wiki/Chernivtsi_urban_hromada "Chernivtsi urban hromada"),[[5]](#cite_note-reform2020-5) the [Chernivtsi Raion](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Raion "Chernivtsi Raion"), and the [oblast itself](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast"). The Chernivtsi population is 264,298 (2022 estimate),[[6]](#cite_note-ua2022estimate-6) and the latest [census in 2001](/wiki/Ukrainian_Census_(2001) "Ukrainian Census (2001)") was 240,600.[[7]](#cite_note-Census-7)

The first document that refers to this city dates back to 1408,[[8]](#cite_note-8) when Chernivtsi was a town in the region of [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"). Initially a defensive fortification, it became the center of Bukovina in 1488. In 1538, the [Principality of Moldavia](/wiki/Principality_of_Moldavia "Principality of Moldavia") came under [Polish](/wiki/Crown_of_the_Kingdom_of_Poland "Crown of the Kingdom of Poland") suzerainty. Later it came under the rule of [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), formally remaining under Moldavian control until 1774, when [Austria](/wiki/Archduchy_of_Austria "Archduchy of Austria") took control of Bukovina in the aftermath of the [Russo-Turkish War](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1768%E2%80%931774) "Russo-Turkish War (1768–1774)").[[9]](#cite_note-9) Until 1848 Chernivtsi (known at that time as *Czernowitz*) was the center of [Galicia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria")'s [Bukovina District](/wiki/Bukovina_District "Bukovina District"), and afterwards served as the capital of the [Duchy of Bukovina](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bukovina "Duchy of Bukovina"). In the aftermath of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), [Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") united with Bukovina in 1918, which led to the city regaining its Romanian name of *Cernăuți*; this lasted until the [Soviets](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") [occupied](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina") Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina. Chernivtsi was under the control of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") from 1940 to 1941, after which Romania recovered the city, and then again from 1944 until the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), after which it became part of [independent Ukraine](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_Ukraine "Declaration of Independence of Ukraine").

Chernivtsi is viewed as one of Western Ukraine's main cultural centers. The city is also considered one of Ukraine's important educational and architectural sites. Historically a cosmopolitan community, Chernivtsi was once dubbed "Little [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna")"[[1]](#cite_note-day-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cognita-2) and "[Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") upon the [Prut](/wiki/Prut "Prut")". The city is a major regional rail and road transportation hub, also housing an [international airport](/wiki/Chernivtsi_International_Airport "Chernivtsi International Airport").

## Names and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Names and etymology")]

See also: [Names of Chernivtsi in different languages](/wiki/Names_of_Chernivtsi_in_different_languages "Names of Chernivtsi in different languages")

Late medieval accounts refer to what was then a [Galicia–Volhynian](/wiki/Galicia%E2%80%93Volhynia "Galicia–Volhynia") fortress-city as *Chern'*, or "the black city"; it is said to owe its name to the black color of the city walls, built from dark [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") layered with [local black-colored soil](/wiki/Chernozem "Chernozem").[[10]](#cite_note-city.cv.ua-10)

In "Documents of Western Russia" ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Акты Западной России) published in Saint Petersburg in 1846 (Volume 1, page 32, document #21), the city is mentioned as *Chernov'tsi* ([Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Черновьци).

Aside from its modern Ukrainian name of Chernivtsi, the city has also been known by several different names in various languages, which still are used by the respective population groups much as they used to be throughout the city's history, either in connection with the rule by one country or another or independently from it:

* [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Cernăuți* ([[tʃernəˈutsʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ro-Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi.ogg "File:Ro-Cernăuți.ogg"));[[11]](#cite_note-KNAB-11)
* [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Czernowitz* ([[ˈtʃɛɐ̯novɪts]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Czernowitz.ogg "File:De-Czernowitz.ogg"));[[11]](#cite_note-KNAB-11)
* [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"): *Czerniowce* ([[/t͡ʂɛrˈɲɔf.t͡sɛ/]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Polish "Help:IPA/Polish") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Pl-Czerniowce.ogg "File:Pl-Czerniowce.ogg"));[[11]](#cite_note-KNAB-11)
* [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *Csernovic*;
* [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"): טשערנאָוויץ, [romanized](/wiki/Romanized "Romanized"): *Tshernovits*;
* [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): Черновцы́, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *Chernovtsy*, (in Russian until 1944: Чернови́цы, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *Chernovitsy*);[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[11]](#cite_note-KNAB-11)
* [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_language "Ukrainian language"): Чернівці, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Chernivtsi*, (in Ukrainian until 1944: Черновиці, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ukrainian "Romanization of Ukrainian"): *Chernovytsi*);[[11]](#cite_note-KNAB-11)

In English, the city was usually referred to as *Czernowitz* before 1918 (under Austrian rule), *Cernăuți* from 1918 to 1944 (Romanian rule) and *Chernovtsy* from 1944 to 1991 (Soviet rule).

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

Archaeological evidence discovered in the area surrounding Chernivtsi indicates that a population inhabited it since the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") era. Later settlements included those of the [Cucuteni-Trypillian culture](/wiki/Cucuteni-Trypillian_culture "Cucuteni-Trypillian culture"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) the [Corded Ware culture](/wiki/Corded_Ware_culture "Corded Ware culture"); artifacts from the [Bronze](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") and [Iron Ages](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") were also found in the city. In the Middle Ages there lived East Slavic tribes [White Croats](/wiki/White_Croats "White Croats") and [Tivertsi](/wiki/Tivertsi "Tivertsi").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Under Principality of Halych

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Under Principality of Halych")]

A fortified settlement located on the left (north-eastern) shore of the Prut in a modern-day Chernivtsi dates back to the time of the [Principality of Halych](/wiki/Galicia-Volhynia "Galicia-Volhynia") and is thought to have been built by [Grand Prince](/wiki/Knyaz "Knyaz") [Yaroslav Osmomysl](/wiki/Yaroslav_Osmomysl "Yaroslav Osmomysl").[[15]](#cite_note-hist-15) This early stronghold was destroyed during the [Mongol invasion of Europe](/wiki/Mongol_invasion_of_Europe "Mongol invasion of Europe") by [Boroldai](/wiki/Boroldai "Boroldai") in 1259. However, the remaining ramparts of the fortress were still used for defense purposes; in the 17th century they were augmented with several bastions, one of which is still extant.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Following the destruction of the fortress, later settlements in the area centered on the right (south-western) shore of the Prut River, at a more strategically advantageous, elevated location. In 1325, when the [Kingdom of Poland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Poland_(1025%E2%80%931385) "Kingdom of Poland (1025–1385)") seized control of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Central_Europe) "Galicia (Central Europe)"), and came into contact with the early [Vlach](/wiki/Vlachs "Vlachs") ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanians "Romanians")) feudal formations, a fort was mentioned under the name *Țețina*; it was defending the ford and crossing point on the [Prut River](/wiki/Prut_River "Prut River"). It was part of a group of three fortifications; the other two being the fortress of [Hotin](/wiki/Khotyn "Khotyn") on the Dniester to the east, and a fort on the [Kolachin River](/w/index.php?title=Kolachin_River&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kolachin River (page does not exist)"), an upriver tributary of Prut.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Under the Principality of Moldavia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Under the Principality of Moldavia")]

See also: [Battle of Cernăuți](/wiki/Battle_of_Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "Battle of Cernăuți")

Between 1359 and 1775, the city and its surroundings were part of the [Principality of Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia"), one of the historic provinces of [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"); the city being the administrative center of the [homonymous *ținut* (county)](/wiki/Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi_County "Cernăuți County").[[16]](#cite_note-16) The name Cernăuți is first attested in a document by [Alexandru cel Bun (Alexander the Good)](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Moldavia "Alexander I of Moldavia") on 8 October 1408.[[17]](#cite_note-17) In [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") sources, the city was mentioned as "Çernovi".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Under Austro-Hungarian rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Under Austro-Hungarian rule")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Greater_austria_ethnic.svg/250px-Greater_austria_ethnic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greater_austria_ethnic.svg)

Map of the *[United States of Greater Austria](/wiki/United_States_of_Greater_Austria "United States of Greater Austria")*, proposed in 1906, shows the city at the border of the areas inhabited by Romanians and Ukrainians.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Wappen_der_Stadt_Czernowitz_1908.png/120px-Wappen_der_Stadt_Czernowitz_1908.png)](/wiki/File:Wappen_der_Stadt_Czernowitz_1908.png)

The city's coat of arms until 1918

In 1775, the northwestern part of the territory of Moldavia was annexed by the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"); this region became known as [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"). The city became the region's capital, organized as the [Bukovina District](/wiki/Bukovina_District "Bukovina District") part of the [Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Galicia_and_Lodomeria "Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria"), which in 1849 was raised in status and became known as the [Duchy of Bukovina](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bukovina "Duchy of Bukovina"), a [crownland](/wiki/Cisleithania#Crown_lands "Cisleithania") of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"). The city received [Magdeburg rights](/wiki/Magdeburg_rights "Magdeburg rights").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The city began to flourish in 1778 when Knight [Karl von Enzenberg](/w/index.php?title=Karl_von_Enzenberg&action=edit&redlink=1 "Karl von Enzenberg (page does not exist)") was appointed the chief of the Military Administration. He invited many merchants, craftsmen and entrepreneurs to help develop trade and other businesses. [Saint Peter](/wiki/Saint_Peter "Saint Peter")'s Fairs (1–15 July) had given a new vibrant impulse to the market development from 1786. In the late 19th century the German language—due to the Habsburg and the very important Jewish influence—became the lingua franca and more and more newspapers were edited in German, also a remarkable literary production in German began in this period, featuring most prominently [Karl Emil Franzos](/wiki/Karl_Emil_Franzos "Karl Emil Franzos").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

During the [Moldavian Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Moldavian_Revolution_of_1848 "Moldavian Revolution of 1848"), while meeting in Czernowitz, Moldavian liberals formed *Comitetul Revoluţionar Moldovean* (the Moldavian Revolutionary Committee) and commissioned [Mihail Kogălniceanu](/wiki/Mihail_Kog%C4%83lniceanu "Mihail Kogălniceanu") to draw up a new statement of principles, *[Dorinţele partidei naţionale din Moldova](/w/index.php?title=Dorin%C5%A3ele_partidei_na%C5%A3ionale_din_Moldova&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dorinţele partidei naţionale din Moldova (page does not exist)")* ("The Wishes of the National Party in Moldavia"), published in August. More liberal than the 9 April petition, it called for an elected assembly with extensive powers, including the right to initiate legislation, and expanded the local autonomy of *[judeţe](/wiki/Jude%C5%A3 "Judeţ")*, cities and rural communes. Kogălniceanu also drafted a constitution, *Proiectul de Constituţie*, which rendered the legislature the dominant branch of government, allowing it to vote taxes, draw up the annual state budget, stimulate agriculture, industry and commerce, reform laws, elect the prince, and choose the [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") and bishops of the [Orthodox Church](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church"). Kogălniceanu, a future [Prime Minister of Romania](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Romania "Prime Minister of Romania"), proposed that all orders of society be represented in the assembly, without calling for universal suffrage. Instead he proposed the creation of electoral college, giving the upper classes predominant power. Like most of his colleagues, he felt obliged to remain mindful of his era's social and political realities by recognizing the *boyars* continued leading role and limiting the participation of peasants due to their lack of education and experience. Among the Moldavian exiles in Czernowitz were also [Vasile Alecsandri](/wiki/Vasile_Alecsandri "Vasile Alecsandri"), [Gheorghe Sion](/wiki/Gheorghe_Sion "Gheorghe Sion") and [Alexandru Ioan Cuza](/wiki/Alexandru_Ioan_Cuza "Alexandru Ioan Cuza").[[20]](#cite_note-Historia_2025-11-20-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Moldova650.asm.md/node/53_2025-11-20-21)

### West Ukrainian People's Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: West Ukrainian People's Republic")]

During the 19th and early 20th century, Chernivtsi became a center of both Ukrainian and Romanian national movements. In 1908, it was the site of the first [Yiddish language](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language") conference, the [Czernowitz Conference](/wiki/Czernowitz_Conference "Czernowitz Conference"), coordinated by [Nathan Birnbaum](/wiki/Nathan_Birnbaum "Nathan Birnbaum"). In 1910, Romanians and Ukrainians were almost in equal numbers with the Romanians concentrated mainly in the south and the Ukrainians mainly in the north.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-22) When [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") dissolved in 1918 after its defeat in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), two years of political uncertainty followed. During the short period of time, [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic"), was proclaimed in [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv") on 1 November 1918. Prior to that, on 14/27th Of october, 1918 Ukrainian Bukovina executive committee was created, a constituent assembly to whom the Austrian governor of the province subsequently handed power.[[23]](#cite_note-Entry_Display_Web_Page_2021-23) In November, committee took power and controlled the Ukrainian part of Bukovina, consisting of Northern Bukovina, and including the city of Chernivtsi, [Zastavna](/wiki/Zastavna "Zastavna"), [Vashkivtsi](/wiki/Vashkivtsi "Vashkivtsi"), [Vyzhnytsia](/wiki/Vyzhnytsia "Vyzhnytsia").[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-Entry_Display_Web_Page_2021-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Sitz_der_Landesregierung_des_Herzogtums_Bukowina_nach_1905.png/250px-Sitz_der_Landesregierung_des_Herzogtums_Bukowina_nach_1905.png)](/wiki/File:Sitz_der_Landesregierung_des_Herzogtums_Bukowina_nach_1905.png)

Building of Ukrainian Bukovina Regional Committee, former Seat of the state government of the Duchy of Bukovina

On November 3, 1918, a large [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine") [Bukovina Viche](/w/index.php?title=%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B5_%D0%B2%D1%96%D1%87%D0%B5&action=edit&redlink=1 "Буковинське віче (page does not exist)") was held in Chernivtsi, at which the majority voted for reunification with the [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic") proclaimed in [Lviv](/wiki/Lviv "Lviv"). It has further unanimously expressed a strong protest against the annexation of Bukovina to the Kingdom of Romania, and elected [Omelian Popovych](/w/index.php?title=%D0%9F%D0%BE%D0%BF%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87_%D0%9E%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%BB%D1%8F%D0%BD_%D0%9E%D0%BB%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%87&action=edit&redlink=1 "Попович Омелян Олександрович (page does not exist)") as a chairman of the Ukrainian Regional Committee.[[25]](#cite_note-25) On November 6, 1918, [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians") took over power in Chernivtsi, [Yosyp Bezpalko](/wiki/Yosyp_Bezpalko "Yosyp Bezpalko") was appointed as a mayor of the city.[[26]](#cite_note-26) At that time, a 2.5 thousand corps of [Ukrainian Sich Riflemen](/wiki/Ukrainian_Sich_Riflemen "Ukrainian Sich Riflemen") were stationed in the city.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Map_of_Ukraine_for_Paris_Peace_Conference.svg/250px-Map_of_Ukraine_for_Paris_Peace_Conference.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Ukraine_for_Paris_Peace_Conference.svg)

Map of Ukraine for Paris Peace Conference, Showing Chernivtsi as territory of [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic")

### Chernivtsi under Romanian rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Chernivtsi under Romanian rule")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Interbelic_Cernauti_CoA.png/120px-Interbelic_Cernauti_CoA.png)](/wiki/File:Interbelic_Cernauti_CoA.png)

The city's coat of arms from 1918 to 1940 and from 1941 to 1944

From 11 November to 19 December 1918, Romanian army takes over Chernivtsi (Cernăuți) and formalizes Bukovina's annexation.[[28]](#cite_note-28) As the aftermath of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Bukovina together with Chernivtsi was [recognised](/wiki/Union_of_Bukovina_with_Romania "Union of Bukovina with Romania") and became a part of the [Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania"), which gained worldwide diplomatic recognition by the end of 1920 as an aftermath of unsuccessful [Ukrainian War of Independence](/wiki/Ukrainian_War_of_Independence "Ukrainian War of Independence"), as [West Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/West_Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "West Ukrainian People's Republic") was [crushed down](/wiki/Ukrainian_Death_Triangle "Ukrainian Death Triangle") together with [Ukrainian People's Republic](/wiki/Ukrainian_People%27s_Republic "Ukrainian People's Republic") by its neighbors.[[29]](#cite_note-eb-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) During those two years, even most city residents did not know of which country they were citizens, with most assuming Czernowitz still belonged to Austria-Hungary.[[31]](#cite_note-31) German remained the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of the city and its suburbs for another decade. In 1930, the city reached a population of 112,400.[[32]](#cite_note-32) While Romanians formed the majority of Bukovina's diverse population in 1930, the ethnic composition of Chernivtsi County had a Ukrainian majority. Ukrainians made up 44.5% of the population, followed by Romanians at 25.5%, Jews at 16.7%, Germans at 6.4%, and Poles at 4.9%. The remaining population consisted of Russians, Romani, Hungarians, and other smaller ethnic groups.[[22]](#cite_note-:2-22)

### Soviet occupation and World War 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Soviet occupation and World War 2")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/%D0%92%D1%81%D1%82%D1%83%D0%BF_%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%90%D1%80%D0%BC%D1%96%D1%97_%D0%B4%D0%BE_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96%D0%B2_1940_%D1%80.jpg/250px-%D0%92%D1%81%D1%82%D1%83%D0%BF_%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%90%D1%80%D0%BC%D1%96%D1%97_%D0%B4%D0%BE_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96%D0%B2_1940_%D1%80.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%92%D1%81%D1%82%D1%83%D0%BF_%D1%87%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%82%D0%B8%D0%BD_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B2%D0%BE%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%90%D1%80%D0%BC%D1%96%D1%97_%D0%B4%D0%BE_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96%D0%B2_1940_%D1%80.jpg)

Soviet occupation troops entering Chernivtsi in 1940

In 1940, the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") [occupied the area](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_Bessarabia_and_Northern_Bukovina "Soviet occupation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina"); the area around the city became known as [Chernivtsi Oblast](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Oblast "Chernivtsi Oblast"), and was allotted to the [Ukrainian SSR](/wiki/Ukrainian_SSR "Ukrainian SSR") by the Soviet Union.[[29]](#cite_note-eb-29) The city's large Romanian intelligentsia found refuge in Romania and those that remained faced repression; while the Bukovina Germans were "repatriated" according to a Soviet-Nazi agreement. During a meeting of the Central Committee [All-Union Communist Party](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") held on 9 September 1940, [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") stated: "I like the city, Chernivtsi. I've been there, I've seen it."[[33]](#cite_note-buktour.icu-33) Stalin most likely visited the city during the Austro-Hungarian rule.[[33]](#cite_note-buktour.icu-33) On June 13, 1941, 8,374 people (mostly Romanians) were deported from the Chernivtsi oblast of Ukraine and 3,767 from the Izmail oblast of Ukraine (southern Bessarabia).[[34]](#cite_note-Bougai153-34) According to Nikolai Bougai's research in the Soviet archives, out of those deported from the Chernivtsi and Izmail oblasts, only 9,595 (79.03%) were still alive in September 1941.[[35]](#cite_note-35) While he did not list any precise data for the city of Chernivtsi or Chernivtsi oblast, only 1,136 of those deported from the Izmail oblast (30.16%) of Soviet Ukraine were still alive in 1951.[[36]](#cite_note-36) According to some sources, a majority of the deportees of 1941 died in the Soviet north and east.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Under the regime of military dictator [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu"), Romania had switched from an ally of France and Britain to one of Nazi Germany; subsequently, in July 1941, the Romanian Army retook the city as part of the [Axis attack on the Soviet Union](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") during World War II. Chernivtsi would become the capital of the Romanian [Bukovina Governorate](/wiki/Bukovina_Governorate "Bukovina Governorate").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/GUVERNAMANTUL_BUCOVINEI.png/250px-GUVERNAMANTUL_BUCOVINEI.png)](/wiki/File:GUVERNAMANTUL_BUCOVINEI.png)

Administrative map of the Governorate of Bukovina as of May 1942

In August 1941, Antonescu ordered the creation of a [ghetto](/wiki/Ghetto "Ghetto") in the lowland part of the city, where 50,000 [Bukovina Jews](/wiki/Bukovina_Jews "Bukovina Jews") were crammed, two-thirds of whom would be deported in October 1941 and early 1942 to [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria_(World_War_II) "Transnistria (World War II)"), where the majority of the deportees died. About 60% of the deportees to Transnistria from the city of Chernivtsi perished according to the Jewish Virtual Library.[[39]](#cite_note-39) According to Gali Mir-Tibon, most of the Jews deported from the city of Chernivtsi to Transnistria did not survive.[[40]](#cite_note-40) According to the Yad Vashem database, 19,424 Jews who had lived in Chernivtsi before the war whose names are listed died in the Holocaust.[[41]](#cite_note-41) According to the Yad Vashem database, the number of Jews who had lived in Chernivtsi before the war and were killed in the city during the Holocaust was 2,478.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The number of Jews who were killed in the city of Chernivtsi itself, regardless of the place of prewar residence, was 3,372 according to the Yad Vashem database, with names included.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Most of these Jews who died in the city were killed by the German Einsatzgruppe D.[[44]](#cite_note-See_Jean_Ancel_1990_p._287-44) The total number of Chernivtsi Jews killed by the SS (Einsatzgruppe D) in July and August 1941 was 3,106 according to the [Einsatzgruppen reports](/wiki/Einsatzgruppen_reports "Einsatzgruppen reports"), while the Romanians killed 400, though survivor testimonies suggest that perhaps more people were killed.[[45]](#cite_note-45) According to Ruth Glasberg, a survivor and eyewitness of the massacre and of the deportations to Transnistria, "thousands of people were killed in the street and in houses by the Germans and Legionaires (i.e., members and supporters of the fascist Legion of the Archangel Michael).[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The Romanian mayor of the city [Traian Popovici](/wiki/Traian_Popovici "Traian Popovici") managed to persuade [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") to raise the number of Jews exempted from deportation from 200 to 20,000.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) Dorimedont Popovici (see <https://ro.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dorimedont_Popovici> ), the uncle of Traian Popovici, the vice-president of the National Council of Bukovina, member of the Romanian parliament and cabinet minister, originally from the People's Party and then from the National Peasant Party, also intervened with Ion Antonescu for the stopping of the deportations of Jews from Bukovina and other things through a 74 page memorandum; there were also other similar interventions.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Another major figure who intervened in favor of the stopping of the deportations of Jews to Transnistria was the Metropolitan of Bukovina, Tit Simedrea (see <https://ro.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tit_Simedrea>), as it was noted in the first major published book on the Holocaust in English, citing the Chief Rabbi of Romania, [Alexandru Safran](/wiki/Alexandru_%C8%98afran "Alexandru Șafran").[[50]](#cite_note-50) In October and November 1941, 28,391 Jews from the city were deported to Transnistria.[[44]](#cite_note-See_Jean_Ancel_1990_p._287-44) About 1,500 Jews from Chernivtsi converted to Christianity to be saved from deportation to Transnistria.[[51]](#cite_note-51) All the Jews deported from Chernivtsi in 1941, and all of those deported in 1942, were deported by train; half of the transports sent out in 1941, and all the ones from 1942, went to [Otaci](/wiki/Otaci "Otaci") (Atachi at that time), typically middle class people, many of whom were able to take lei and valuables into Transnistria (just like the southern Bukovinian Jews), directly on the border with Transnistria, from where they crossed the border into Transnistria, and were not beaten and forced to buy food at high prices in a transit camp.[[52]](#cite_note-Radu_Ioanid_2000_p._137-138-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Jean_Ancel_2011_p._279-280-53) The other half, mostly working class individuals, were sent to the transit camp in Marculesti transit camp, where most gave up much of their jewelry and precious metals in exchange for food, and were often beaten and plundered by Romanian gendarmes, and from where they walked to nearby Atachi to be sent across the river to Transnistria.[[52]](#cite_note-Radu_Ioanid_2000_p._137-138-52)[[53]](#cite_note-Jean_Ancel_2011_p._279-280-53) A further 4,290 Chernivtsi Jews were deported to Transnistria in June 1942.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) About 16,794 of the Jews were allowed to stay in Chernivtsi after that.[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Besides the deportations of Jews to Transnistria in June 1942, Bukovina's governor Corneliu Calotescu reported to Ion Antonescu on August 21, 1942, that 147 Jewish Communists were deported to Transnistria, mainly from the city of Chernivtsi; they were not included in the statistics of deported Jews.[[58]](#cite_note-58) One of the Jews who remained in the city was Erich Goldhagen (see the page in German at <https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Erich_Goldhagen>), the father of [Daniel Jonah Goldhagen](/wiki/Daniel_Goldhagen "Daniel Goldhagen").[[59]](#cite_note-59) On March 14, 1944, Romania's military dictator Ion Antonescu allowed the repatriation of all the Jews deported to Transnistria.[[60]](#cite_note-60) For more information on the Holocaust in [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria "Transnistria"), including on the fate of the Jewish deportees from Chernivtsi, see [History of the Jews in Transnistria](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Transnistria "History of the Jews in Transnistria"). For more information on the history of the Jews of Bukovina, including during the Holocaust, see [History of the Jews in Bukovina](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Bukovina "History of the Jews in Bukovina").

After 29 March 1944, when [Axis forces](/wiki/Axis_forces "Axis forces") were driven out by the Red Army, the city was reincorporated into the Ukrainian SSR. Over the following years, most of the Jews emigrated to Israel; the city was an important node in the [Berihah](/wiki/Berihah "Berihah") network. Bukovina Poles were expelled by the Soviets after World War II. The city became a predominantly Ukrainian one.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Independence")]

Since 1991, Chernivtsi has been a part of an independent Ukraine. In May 1999, Romania opened a [consulate general](/wiki/Consulate_general "Consulate general") in the city.

Until 18 July 2020, Chernivtsi was designated as a [city of oblast significance](/wiki/City_of_regional_significance_(Ukraine) "City of regional significance (Ukraine)") and did not belong to any raion. As part of the administrative reform of Ukraine, which reduced the number of raions of Chernivtsi Oblast to three, the city was merged into Chernivtsi Raion.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Russian invasion of Ukraine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Russian invasion of Ukraine")]

See also: [Ukrainian refugee crisis](/wiki/Ukrainian_refugee_crisis "Ukrainian refugee crisis")

Since the start of the [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the city has been a host for refugees from the fighting in eastern and central Ukraine[[63]](#cite_note-Aljazeera-63) and a resting point for refugees on their way to nearby [Romania](/wiki/2022_Ukrainian_refugee_crisis#Romania "2022 Ukrainian refugee crisis").[[63]](#cite_note-Aljazeera-63) Some Chernivtsi residents have also left the country.

### Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

The Chernivtsi coat of arms is framed by a bronze ornamental cartouche, and a red heraldic shield depicting an open stone gate with a figured trident in the middle. Under the gate, there are two crossed laurel branches, tied with ribbons. The crown of the symbol is the stone crown.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The Chernivtsi flag consists of a tree, the top, and a rectangular cloth, the front of which forms framed by a red tooth-like ornament white background with an inscription in Ukrainian in the center, over which there is inscribed in Ukrainian: "Chernivtsi". Under the coat of arms, there is the sign "1408" – the date of the first written mention of the city. On both sides of the coat of arms and all four corners of the field are filled with floral ornaments and with the addition of two beech branches with nuts and leaves. ### Honorary chain of the mayor of Chernivtsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Honorary chain of the mayor of Chernivtsi")]

The mayor's honorary chain is a symbol of Chernivtsi mayor's authority, which is served on behalf of the territorial community. Founded in 1908 and restored in 2008. The symbol is a medallion with the inscription engraved on it: "From Chernivtsi community to freely elected head", on the reverse – "The foundation of a free state is a free community". The medallion is attached to a chain consisting of stylized coats of arms Ukraine, Chernivtsi region and the city of Chernivtsi. The symbol is made of gold colour metal.

The medal "To the glory of Chernivtsi" is an honorary distinction of the [Chernivtsi City Council](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi_City_Council&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chernivtsi City Council (page does not exist)"), introduced to the 600th Anniversary of Chernivtsi (2008) in order to reward individuals who actively contributed to the prosperity of the city and its promotion in Ukraine and the world. The award is made of [silver-gilt](/wiki/Silver-gilt "Silver-gilt"), it has a circle shape with a diameter of 28 mm (1.1 in). The medal's strip is white with red stripes, which corresponds to the colours of the Chernivtsi flag. At the bottom of the strip, there is a beech branch. The obverse depicts the emblem of Chernivtsi and the inscription – "To the glory of Chernivtsi". On the reverse – the official Chernivtsi logo, designed and approved for the anniversary. The medal is awarded, according to the decision of the executive committee of the City Council, annually during the celebration of the city day.

The official motto of modern Chernivtsi, "Спільними зусиллями!", is a Ukrainian-language version of the Latin *Viribus Unitis* ("With United Forces"), the personal motto of [Franz Joseph](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria"), who personally bestowed the right to use it on Chernivtsi. This indicates a special attitude of the emperor to the city. Along with the capital of Bukovina, only the first naval ship of the [Austro-Hungarian Navy](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Navy "Austro-Hungarian Navy") ([SMS *Viribus Unitis*](/wiki/SMS_Viribus_Unitis "SMS Viribus Unitis")) was honoured with such honour.

### Logo of Chernivtsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Logo of Chernivtsi")]

The official ''Chernivtsi 600'' logo was developed and approved by the anniversary of the city in 2008. It was recognized so successful that it continues to be used. The main idea of components for emblem is based on the antiquity of the city, its exceptional architectural heritage and the hard work of its inhabitants. The symbol is made in the form of a blacksmith's work of art, which testifies to the soundness, prosperity, and success. The color scheme of the logo, represented by dark blue and yellow, has a higher degree of comfortable contrast and coincides with the colors of the [State Flag of Ukraine](/wiki/Flag_of_Ukraine "Flag of Ukraine").[[66]](#cite_note-66)

In the early 2010s, a new city logo was developed and approved, and at the same time the official slogan was affixed: "Chernivtsi is unique in diversity". Old and new symbols of Chernivtsi were chosen for its creation. To the left, in the foreground, there is a trumpet player who wins the trumpet tune "Marichka". In the middle of the background, there is the town hall. The former [Metropolitan Residence of Bukovina and Dalmatia](/wiki/Residence_of_Bukovinian_and_Dalmatian_Metropolitans "Residence of Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans") which is recognized as the architectural pearl of the city is pictured to the right in the background.

The colour scheme of the logo represented in orange, blue and red, the name is purple. Such a combination is characteristic of tourism, which uses the notion of happiness, well-being, the joy of relaxation, visualizing positive symbols and images in a colourful, warm and vibrant colour scheme. The new logo uses old symbols from the "Chernivtsi 600" logo.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Geography")]

Chernivtsi is located in the historic region of [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), which is currently shared between Romania (south) and Ukraine (north). Chernivtsi is located in the southwest of Ukraine, in the eastern Carpathians, on the border between the Carpathians and the East European Plain, 40 km (25 mi) from the border with Romania. The city lies 248 meters (817') [above sea level](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level") and is surrounded by forests and fields. The River [Prut](/wiki/Prut "Prut") runs through the city's landscape. The city is located in the Eastern European time zone in the region of meridian 26.

Chernivtsi is located at the intersection of the transport arteries: E85, H03, and H10

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Climate")]

The city is located in a temperate climate zone. The climate is continental with mild winters and warm summers. The average annual temperature is +8.6 °C (47.5 °F), the lowest in January (−2.9 °C; 26.8 °F), the highest – in July (+19.8 °C; 67.6 °F). Winter weather usually comes on 28 November and ends 9 March; summer weather begins on 20 May, and ends on 10 September. The average annual rainfall in Chernivtsi is 621 millimetres (24+1⁄2 in), with the lowest – in October and January–February, the highest – in June–July. Sometimes there are heavy rains during the summer.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Snow cover is formed each winter, but its altitude is insignificant. The average wind speed ranges from 3.3 m/s (7 mph) in July to 4.0 m/s (9 mph) in January. The average annual humidity is 76%.

### Landscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Landscape")]

The total area of Chernivtsi within the administrative boundaries of 2013 is about 153 km2 (59 sq mi). According to the functional purpose the lands of the city are divided as follows: land of residential and public buildings (64%), lands of agricultural purpose (17%), lands of industry (9%), lands of recreational and environmental purpose (5%), lands of general use (3%), commercial land (2%).[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The main water source of Chernivtsi is the [Prut River](/wiki/Prut_river "Prut river") in its upper reaches, which divides the city in half. Besides, there are six small streams and nine lakes within the city.

The relief is characterized by significant relief dip – from 150 metres (490 ft) above sea level in the Prut valleys to 537 metres (1,762 ft) in the western outskirts (Mount [Tsetzino](/w/index.php?title=Tsetzino&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tsetzino (page does not exist)")), which is caused by the location on the [Chernivtsi Upland](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi_Upland&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chernivtsi Upland (page does not exist)").

Chernivtsi is considered to be a "green city": the large territory is occupied by parks, squares, gardens, alleys and flower gardens. Nine objects are recognized as monuments of landscape art. The city has a botanical garden at the Yuriy Fedkovych National University with a unique orangery. Among the relict plants growing in the botanical garden, a special place is occupied by a giant [Sequoiadendron](/wiki/Sequoiadendron "Sequoiadendron").[[71]](#cite_note-71)

Chernivtsi is located in the center of [Chernivtsi Regional Park](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi_Regional_Park&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chernivtsi Regional Park (page does not exist)"), which borders zakaznik "Thetzino" in the west and [Mount Berda](/w/index.php?title=Mount_Berda&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mount Berda (page does not exist)") in the north.

### State of the environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: State of the environment")]

At the end of the twentieth century, the main pollutants of the Chernivtsi environment were industrial enterprises, including the MIC. In the 1990s much of them ceased to exist or significantly reduced production capacity and thus reduced industrial emissions. Despite this, 58 enterprises (38.4% of the total amount in the region) are the main pollutants of the environment. Approximately 1.2 tonnes of pollutants are released into the air annually (34.9% of the total area emissions). Non-methane [volatile organic compounds](/wiki/Volatile_organic_compound "Volatile organic compound"), carbon dioxide and substances in the form of solid suspended solids predominate in the structure of the emitted pollutants. In addition, carbon dioxide, which has a [greenhouse effect](/wiki/Greenhouse_effect "Greenhouse effect"), is periodically released into the atmosphere of the city. Emissions from stationary sources were 7.9 tonnes per 1 km2 (0.39 sq mi) of Chernivtsi territory. Each inhabitant of the regional center accounts for an average of 4.8 kg (11 lb) of harmful emissions per year.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

In 2008, Chernivtsi established an Environmental Monitoring System (EMS), an information structure that integrates environmental monitoring organizations and industrial enterprises that pollute the environment or which may adversely affect the environment or its components.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

Since the late 1990s, transport is a significant factor in the negative impact on the environment. To some extent, the situation was improved with the construction of the first (2004) and the second (2010) queues of the [bypass road](/wiki/Bypass_(road) "Bypass (road)"), which connected the directions "Kyiv-Chernivtsi" and "Chernivtsi-Suceava". The problem of transit transport in the city will be finally resolved after the construction of the third branch of the bypass road, which will connect the directions "Suceava-Chernivtsi" and "Chernivtsi-Lviv".[[74]](#cite_note-74)

The [National Palace of the Romanians of Chernivtsi](/w/index.php?title=National_Palace_of_the_Romanians_of_Chernivtsi&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Palace of the Romanians of Chernivtsi (page does not exist)") [[ro](https://ro.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palatul_Na%C8%9Bional_al_Rom%C3%A2nilor_din_Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "ro:Palatul Național al Românilor din Cernăuți"); [uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC_%D1%83_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%8F%D1%85 "uk:Румунський Народний Дім у Чернівцях")]

The Romanian population in Chernivtsi started decreasing rapidly after 1950. Many Romanians fled to Romania or were deported to [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") (where most of them died according to some sources)[[82]](#cite_note-82), and the remaining Romanian population quickly became a minority and assimilated with the majority.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to minority organizations, the Romanian minority in Chernivtsi is still decreasing nowadays as a result of [cultural assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") and emigration to Romania.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/2019_-_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_-_%D0%84%D0%B2%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B5_%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%89%D0%B5_-_2.jpg/250px-2019_-_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_-_%D0%84%D0%B2%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B5_%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%89%D0%B5_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:2019_-_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%96_-_%D0%84%D0%B2%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B5_%D0%BA%D0%BB%D0%B0%D0%B4%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D1%89%D0%B5_-_2.jpg)

[Jewish cemetery of Chernivtsi](/wiki/Jewish_cemetery_of_Chernivtsi "Jewish cemetery of Chernivtsi")

Chernivtsi once had a Jewish community of over 50,000, many of whom did not survive World War II. About 60% of the Jewish deportees to Transnistria from the city of Chernivtsi died there according to the Jewish Virtual Library.[[84]](#cite_note-84) According to Gali Mir-Tibon, most of the Jews deported from the city of Chernivtsi, as well as northern Bukovina in general, to Transnistria did not survive.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Romanian lawyer and reserve officer [Theodor Criveanu](/w/index.php?title=Theodor_Criveanu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Theodor Criveanu (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%AA%D7%99%D7%90%D7%95%D7%93%D7%95%D7%A8_%D7%A7%D7%A8%D7%99%D7%91%D7%99%D7%90%D7%A0%D7%95 "he:תיאודור קריביאנו"); [ro](https://ro.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theodor_Criveanu "ro:Theodor Criveanu")], as well as the then city mayor [Traian Popovici](/wiki/Traian_Popovici "Traian Popovici"), supported by General [Vasile Ionescu](/wiki/Vasile_Ionescu "Vasile Ionescu") saved 19,689 Jewish people. Initially, Governor of Bukovina [Corneliu Calotescu](/wiki/Corneliu_Calotescu "Corneliu Calotescu") allowed only 190 Jewish people to stay, but Traian Popovici, after an incredible effort, obtained from the then dictator of Romania Marshal [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") an allowance of 20,000.[[86]](#cite_note-86) After World War II, the city was a key node in the [Berihah](/wiki/Berihah "Berihah") network, which helped Jews to emigrate to the then [Mandate Palestine](/wiki/Mandate_Palestine "Mandate Palestine") from the difficult conditions after the War. Following the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991, the majority of the remaining Jewish population emigrated to Israel and the United States. A famous member of this latter emigration is the actress [Mila Kunis](/wiki/Mila_Kunis "Mila Kunis").[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Chernivtsi was inhabited by Ukrainians, Romanians, Poles, [Ruthenians](/wiki/Ruthenians "Ruthenians"), Jews, [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people"), and [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans"). During its affiliation with the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian "Austro-Hungarian") monarchy, Chernivtsi enjoyed prosperity and culture as the capital of the [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") [crown land](/wiki/Crown_land#Austria "Crown land"). Until 1918, the main language of the city was German, which, in addition to the Germans, was also spoken by Jews (together they made up half the population of the city) and even partly by Ukrainians, Romanians and Poles. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [Shoah](/wiki/Shoah "Shoah") and [Porajmos](/wiki/Porajmos "Porajmos"), and the resettlement and expulsion of the whole ethnic groups, including Germans and Romanians, this status was diminished. Today, the Ukrainians are the dominant population group.

Chernivtsi's change in demographic diversity is demonstrated by the following population statistics. Once, Romanians and Ukrainians formed the majority of the population. However, after 1870, [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language")-speaking or [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")-speaking Jews surpassed the Romanians as the largest population group of the town.

The total number of economic entities in the city is 25.4 thousand. On 1 January 2006, there were 6739 legal entities – business entities and almost 19,000 private entrepreneurs – individuals, primarily represented by small enterprises. The volume of sales and services provided to small enterprises is ₴578 million or 22% of the total Chernivtsi volumes. The share of the city's tax revenues is almost 35%. The most attractive for small businesses are trade and services, restaurant and tourist business.

Wholesale and retail trade, industry and construction are successfully developing in Chernivtsi. In 2005, wholesale and retail sales accounted for over 64%, industry – 23%, construction – 6%, real estate operations – 2.3%, transport and communications – more than 2%.

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Industry")]

In the industrial sector of the city, there are 10 branches, which have 70 large enterprises with a total number of employees over 20 thousand people or 13% of the working population of the city. The annual volume of industrial production at these enterprises is about ₴775 million. The share of citywide tax revenues to the budgets of all levels of the industry is 21%. Defining industries in the city's industry are food, light, mechanical engineering and woodworking. Food processing companies produce sugar, bakery products, alcohol, oil, meat and milk, fruits, vegetables and other products. In the light industry, the production of garments, knitwear, hosiery, rubber and leather footwear and textiles prevails. Mechanical engineering is represented by the production of oil and gas processing equipment and agricultural machinery. The timber industry is dominated by the production of lumber, furniture, joinery and other wood products.

### Trade and services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Trade and services")]

In 2005, there were 1922 trade enterprises, 609 restaurants, 892 services in the city. There are 22 markets and micro-markets in the city. ₴10 million are invested annually in their construction, reconstruction, improvement of trade conditions and creation of facilities for buyers. Chernivtsi City Shopping Complex, "Kalinivskiy Rynok" Municipal Enterprise is a modern multidisciplinary enterprise with powerful infrastructure. The average daily number of market visitors is 50,000 people, served by 9,100 entrepreneurs. The volume of services in 2005 amounted to almost ₴23 million, more than ₴18 million was paid into the city budget, or nearly 10% of the total revenues.

## Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Health care")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%83%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%82.Jpg/250px-%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%83%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%82.Jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%91%D1%83%D0%BA%D0%BE%D0%B2%D0%B8%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B6%D0%B0%D0%B2%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%BC%D0%B5%D0%B4%D0%B8%D1%87%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D1%83%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%82.Jpg)

[Bukovinian State Medical University](/wiki/Bukovinian_State_Medical_University "Bukovinian State Medical University")

Almost all health care establishments of the region are concentrated in Chernivtsi. 39 medical establishments (hospitals, clinics, and [polyclinics](/wiki/Polyclinic "Polyclinic")) provide citizens of Chernivtsi with necessary medical care.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Medical services are provided by 4.47 thousand people, of which – 1102 doctors, 1902 – average health workers, 1473 – junior and support staff.

Municipal medical establishments provide the following medical services:

* Emergency care (emergency care station);
* Dispensary and polyclinic care (5 municipal polyclinics, a municipal children polyclinic, polyclinics of two maternity houses, a polyclinic of preventive examination and Municipal Dentistry Association, which includes two dentist clinics);
* Specialized medical care (3 hospitals, 2 maternity houses, a tuberculosis hospital and a municipal children hospital);
* Disease-prevention and anti-epidemic services (a municipal sanitary and epidemiological station).

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Congregation_of_Holy_Cross_Church_%281814%29.jpg/250px-Congregation_of_Holy_Cross_Church_%281814%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Congregation_of_Holy_Cross_Church_(1814).jpg)

Basilica of the Exaltation of the Cross

Throughout the centuries Chernivtsi, as the center of Bukovina, was forming as a multicultural city with an atmosphere of tolerance, which contributed to the development of artists and artistic institutions across the ethnic boundaries.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/%D0%9F%D0%B0%D0%BC%27%D1%8F%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA_%D0%9C%D1%96%D1%85%D0%B0%D1%8E_%D0%95%D0%BC%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D1%83_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B7%D1%96_%D0%A3%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%97_%D1%96_%D0%91%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B8_2.jpg/250px-%D0%9F%D0%B0%D0%BC%27%D1%8F%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA_%D0%9C%D1%96%D1%85%D0%B0%D1%8E_%D0%95%D0%BC%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D1%83_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B7%D1%96_%D0%A3%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%97_%D1%96_%D0%91%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B8_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9F%D0%B0%D0%BC%27%D1%8F%D1%82%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA_%D0%9C%D1%96%D1%85%D0%B0%D1%8E_%D0%95%D0%BC%D1%96%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%81%D0%BA%D1%83_%D0%BD%D0%B0_%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B7%D1%96_%D0%A3%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D0%B5%D1%80%D1%81%D0%B8%D1%82%D0%B5%D1%82%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%BE%D1%97_%D1%96_%D0%91%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%B4%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%B8_2.jpg)

Statue of [Mihai Eminescu](/wiki/Mihai_Eminescu "Mihai Eminescu")

The city has 2 theaters, a philharmonic hall, an organ hall (located in the building of an [Armenian Catholic](/wiki/Armenian_Catholic_Church "Armenian Catholic Church") Church),[[93]](#cite_note-93) more than 10 museums, 6 cinemas, 31 libraries, a palace of culture, 4 music schools and a fine arts school.[[94]](#cite_note-94) The city has more than 100 religious organizations and diocesan authorities. More than a dozen of active non-profit cultural organizations operate in Chernivtsi, including Adam Mickiewicz Polish Culture Society, Mihai Eminescu Romanian Culture Society and the Society of Austrian and German Culture.[[95]](#cite_note-:1-95) The [National Palace of the Romanians of Chernivtsi](/w/index.php?title=National_Palace_of_the_Romanians_of_Chernivtsi&action=edit&redlink=1 "National Palace of the Romanians of Chernivtsi (page does not exist)") [[ro](https://ro.wikipedia.org/wiki/Palatul_Na%C8%9Bional_al_Rom%C3%A2nilor_din_Cern%C4%83u%C8%9Bi "ro:Palatul Național al Românilor din Cernăuți"); [uk](https://uk.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%A0%D1%83%D0%BC%D1%83%D0%BD%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC_%D1%83_%D0%A7%D0%B5%D1%80%D0%BD%D1%96%D0%B2%D1%86%D1%8F%D1%85 "uk:Румунський Народний Дім у Чернівцях")] is a building of great relevance for the ethnic Romanians of the city and the surrounding area.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

Since 1997 Chernivtsi has hosted an international art event under "Days of European Culture Heritage" project. Every year "Bukovinian Meetings" folklore festival is held during the City Day in which art groups from Poland, Hungary, Romania and Germany take part.[[95]](#cite_note-:1-95)

Important part of Chernivtsi cultural life is Malanka Fest, Ukraine's main carnival timed to the religious St. Melania ("[Malanka](/wiki/Malanka "Malanka")") Day and St. Basil Day. The festival is usually held on 14 January, although the date may be moved to match the weekend.[[97]](#cite_note-97) During the Festival groups from different towns and settlements of Bukovina compete in the artistic ingenuity.

One of the biggest [literary festival](/wiki/Literary_festival "Literary festival") in Ukraine is the [Meridian Czernowitz](/w/index.php?title=Meridian_Czernowitz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Meridian Czernowitz (page does not exist)") International Poetic Festival. The purpose of the festival is to return Chernivtsi to the cultural map of Europe and to develop a dialogue between contemporary Ukrainian poets and their foreign colleagues.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The festival saw the participants from more than 13 different countries.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

Several of the publications of [Ukraine's Romanian minority](/wiki/Romanians_in_Ukraine "Romanians in Ukraine") are published in Chernivtsi, including *[Zorile Bucovinei](/wiki/Zorile_Bucovinei "Zorile Bucovinei")*, *Concordia*, *Libertatea Cuvântului*, *Curierul de Cernăuți*, *Arcașul* and *Septentrion literar*. Cernăuți TV, a private Romanian-language TV channel is also headquartered in Chernivtsi.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

Museum of the Diaspora of Bukovina

* [Chernivtsi Museum of Local Lore](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi_Museum_of_Local_Lore&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chernivtsi Museum of Local Lore (page does not exist)") has the largest collection of materials and artifacts of the nature, history, and culture of [Northern Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"): a collection of old printed books with a unique [Ostroh Bible](/wiki/Ostrog_Bible "Ostrog Bible"), printed by [Ivan Fedorov](/wiki/Ivan_Fyodorov_(printer) "Ivan Fyodorov (printer)") in 1581; numismatic collection with more than 3 thousand coins; a collection of weapons; an archaeological collection of more than 12,000 objects. The museum hosts a collection of works of fine and decorative arts, the basis of which consists of icons of the 16th to 18th centuries and works of prominent Bukovinian artists. The natural collection includes nearly 10,000 specimens, including taxidermy, herbarium and entomological collections.
* [Chernivtsi Regional Art Museum](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Regional_Art_Museum "Chernivtsi Regional Art Museum"). The museum is located in an Art Nouveau monument of architecture of national significance. The total number of exhibits in the museum exceeds 10,000, including a collection of unique Bukovinian folk and religious images, rugs and glass icons of the 19th and 20th centuries, Bukovina and [Hutsul](/wiki/Hutsuls "Hutsuls") [pysankas](/wiki/Pysanka "Pysanka"), old printed books, isuch as the 1632 printing of "[The Apostle](/w/index.php?title=The_Apostle_(book)&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Apostle (book) (page does not exist)")" and a number of academic and modernist paintings.[[101]](#cite_note-101)
* [History and Culture Museum of Bukovinian Jews](/w/index.php?title=History_and_Culture_Museum_of_Bukovinian_Jews&action=edit&redlink=1 "History and Culture Museum of Bukovinian Jews (page does not exist)"), located in the former Jewish People's House (now the Central Palace of Culture).[[102]](#cite_note-102) The museum hosts a collection of exhibits on the religious and secular lives of Bukovina Jewish community from 18th to the middle of the 20th century, including books, documents, photographs, costumes and decorative arts.
* [Chernivtsi Museum of Folk Architecture and Folkways](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi_Museum_of_Folk_Architecture_and_Folkways&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chernivtsi Museum of Folk Architecture and Folkways (page does not exist)"), an architectural and landscape complex consisting of monuments of folk architecture of the late 18th to the first half of the 20th centuries.[[103]](#cite_note-103) The exposition of the museum includes about 35 structures, transported from different parts of the region and rebuilt in their original forms with appropriate natural surroundings.
* [Olha Kobylianska Literary Memorial Museum](/w/index.php?title=Olha_Kobylyanska_Literary_Memorial_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Olha Kobylyanska Literary Memorial Museum (page does not exist)")

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Architecture")]

There are many places which attract residents and visitors of Chernivtsi: Drama Theatre, Regional Philharmonic Society, Organ and Chamber Music Hall, puppet theatre, Museum of Local Lore, History and Economy, Museum of Fine Arts, Bukovinian Diaspora Museum, Museum of Folk Architecture and Way of Life, memorial museums of writers, the Central Palace of Culture, the Star Alley in Teatralna Square.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/%D0%90%D1%80%D1%85%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B1%D0%BB%D1%8C_%D0%A2%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%BF%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%89%D1%96.Jpg/960px-%D0%90%D1%80%D1%85%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B1%D0%BB%D1%8C_%D0%A2%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%BF%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%89%D1%96.Jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%90%D1%80%D1%85%D1%96%D1%82%D0%B5%D0%BA%D1%82%D1%83%D1%80%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%B0%D0%BD%D1%81%D0%B0%D0%BC%D0%B1%D0%BB%D1%8C_%D0%A2%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%BE%D1%97_%D0%BF%D0%BB%D0%BE%D1%89%D1%96.Jpg)

Teatralna Square

The city of Chernivtsi has a lot of architecturally important buildings. Many historic buildings have been preserved, especially within the city's center.

As Chernivtsi was part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire, it was closely related to the empire's culture, including architecture. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Main architectural styles present within the city include [Vienna Secession](/wiki/Vienna_Secession "Vienna Secession") and [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism"), [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture"), late [Gothic architecture](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture"), and fragments of traditional Ukrainian and Moldavian architecture, [Byzantine architecture](/wiki/Byzantine_architecture "Byzantine architecture") as well as [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism").[[104]](#cite_note-chernivtsi-sports2-104) During the Interwar Romanian administration, a great number of buildings in the Neo-Romanian and Art Deco architectural styles were also built. The city is sometimes dubbed *Little Vienna*, because its architecture is reminiscent of the Austro-Hungarian capital [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna").[[1]](#cite_note-day-1)[[2]](#cite_note-cognita-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Chern-Panorama1.jpg/960px-Chern-Panorama1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chern-Panorama1.jpg)

Tsentralna Square

The main architectural attractions of the city include: [Olha Kobylianska Chernivtsi Drama Theater](/wiki/Olha_Kobylianska_Chernivtsi_Drama_Theater "Olha Kobylianska Chernivtsi Drama Theater") (1905); [Chernivtsi Town Hall](/wiki/Chernivtsi_Town_Hall "Chernivtsi Town Hall") (1847); the [Residence of Bukovinian and Damatian Metropolitans](/wiki/Residence_of_Bukovinian_and_Dalmatian_Metropolitans "Residence of Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans") (the building of [Chernivtsi University](/wiki/Chernivtsi_University "Chernivtsi University"))—[UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Ukraine "List of World Heritage Sites in Ukraine") (1882); [Chernivtsi University Botanical Garden](/wiki/Chernivtsi_National_University_Botanical_Garden "Chernivtsi National University Botanical Garden") (1877); the Regional Museum of Fine Arts—the former savings bank (1900); the building of the Regional Council—former Palace of Justice (1906); and the Chernivtsi Palace of Culture—former Jewish National House (1908); among many others. The [Moorish Revival](/wiki/Moorish_Revival "Moorish Revival") [Czernowitz Synagogue](/wiki/Czernowitz_Synagogue "Czernowitz Synagogue") was heavily damaged by fire in 1941, the walls were used to create the "Chernivtsi" movie theater.[[105]](#cite_note-Danilova2012-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Chernivtsi_University.jpg/960px-Chernivtsi_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chernivtsi_University.jpg)

Chernivtsi University

The [Czech](/wiki/Czechs "Czechs") architect [Josef Hlávka](/wiki/Josef_Hl%C3%A1vka "Josef Hlávka") designed, in 1864–1882, the buildings that currently house the [Chernivtsi State University](/wiki/Chernivtsi_University "Chernivtsi University"). They were originally the residence of the Bukovinian and Dalmatian Metropolitans. The Romanesque and Byzantine architecture is embellished with Ukrainian traditional architecture and [motifs](/wiki/Motif_(visual_arts) "Motif (visual arts)") of Ukrainian folk art; for example, the tile roof patterns duplicate the geometric designs of traditional [Ukrainian embroidery](/wiki/Ukrainian_embroidery "Ukrainian embroidery").

### Polish National House in Chernivtsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Polish National House in Chernivtsi")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/HerrenGasse_761.jpg/250px-HerrenGasse_761.jpg)](/wiki/File:HerrenGasse_761.jpg)

Polish House in Chernivtsi

The history of the Polish community in Chernivtsi dates back to the late 18th century, when authorities of the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") encouraged Poles to move to [Bucovina](/wiki/Bucovina "Bucovina"). By the mid-19th century, several Polish organizations existed in the city, including Bratnia Pomoc (Brotherly Aid) and Czytelnia Polska (Polish Reading Room). On the initiative of publishers of the Gazeta Polska daily newspaper, collection of money for the construction of Polish House was initiated.[[106]](#cite_note-106) In the early 20th century, two Polish activists, doctor Tadeusz Mischke and judge Jakub Simonowicz purchased a house. In 1904, its expansion was initiated. It was carried out by architect Franciszek Skowron, interior decorator Konrad Górecki and sculptors from [Zakopane](/wiki/Zakopane "Zakopane"), Skwarnicki and Gerasimowicz. The expansion was completed in 1905, and Polish House operated until [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1945, Soviet authorities opened here a cinema, later a music school. Currently, the complex houses Adam Mickiewicz Association of Polish Culture.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Apart from the Polish House, Chernivtsi also has German, Romanian and Jewish Houses.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### German National House in Chernivtsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: German National House in Chernivtsi")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/%D0%9D%D1%96%D0%BC%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC.jpg/250px-%D0%9D%D1%96%D0%BC%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%9D%D1%96%D0%BC%D0%B5%D1%86%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC.jpg)

German House in Chernivtsi

It was built in the early 20th century by the union of the German community in Chernivtsi, which became the center of German cultural and social life in Chernivtsi and Bukovina.[[107]](#cite_note-107) The German House was built in 1908–1910 according to plans developed by architect Gustav Fric.[[108]](#cite_note-108) The building measures 1700 square metres, 25,000 cubic metres. built as a profitable house and a partnership house for 700,000 kroons on the site of the old German school building. The German House also had its own bank, and its own printing house, where various books, brochures, newspapers, and magazines were published, including the newspaper "German diary", which was popular at the time.

### Jewish National House in Chernivtsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Chernivtsi&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Jewish National House in Chernivtsi")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/%D0%84%D0%B2%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC_DSC_8863_%D0%BF%D0%BB._%D0%A2%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B0%2C_6.jpg/250px-%D0%84%D0%B2%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC_DSC_8863_%D0%BF%D0%BB._%D0%A2%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B0%2C_6.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D0%84%D0%B2%D1%80%D0%B5%D0%B9%D1%81%D1%8C%D0%BA%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%9D%D0%B0%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B4%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%B9_%D0%94%D1%96%D0%BC_DSC_8863_%D0%BF%D0%BB._%D0%A2%D0%B5%D0%B0%D1%82%D1%80%D0%B0%D0%BB%D1%8C%D0%BD%D0%B0,_6.jpg)

The Jewish National House in recent years.

See also: [History of the Jews in Chernivtsi](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Chernivtsi "History of the Jews in Chernivtsi")

The house was built in 1908 by the Jewish community and until the Second World War, it was the centre of Jewish life in Chernivtsi and home to various Jewish associations and organisations. At least 45,000 Jews from the Bukovina region fell victim to mass shootings, forced labour and deportations beginning in 1941.[[109]](#cite_note-109) With the advent of the Soviet government (1944), the building was transferred to the City House of Culture. Today it is the Central Palace of Culture of Chernivtsi
